skill.name: enact-hello-go
skill.description: Runs a small Go greeting example that accepts a name and prints the Go runtime version.
skill.summary: Run a minimal Go greeting tool from an Enact skill.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported dynamic import, shell backtick, and weak cryptography patterns, but those specific hits are false positives in the reviewed files. The real concern is the skill command that interpolates the user-supplied name into a Go command string, which may be risky if the runner invokes it through a shell without safe argument handling.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Command String
medium_findings.description: The skill command passes the name input through `${name}` in `go run /workspace/hello.go ${name}`. If the runner executes this command through a shell or does not quote arguments safely, crafted input could change command behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command interpolation is directly visible, but exploitability depends on Enact runner argument handling. No malicious intent or network exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Go Import Block
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged a dynamic import expression, but the location is a normal Go import block for fmt, os, and runtime packages. This is not dynamic code loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is standard Go source code and contains only static standard library imports. There is no import() expression or runtime loading behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Usage Fence
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged shell backtick execution, but the location is a fenced bash usage example in documentation. It is not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside a Markdown code fence showing how to run the example. It is documentation, not runtime code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns in metadata descriptions. The reviewed lines describe a greeting tool and input field, and no cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are plain YAML description strings. I found no hash function, cipher, key handling, or crypto library use in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command String Input Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: The command template embeds the user-controlled name value directly into a command string. Prefer structured argument passing or strict validation for command parameters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is explicit in the command template. The actual security impact depends on how the Enact runtime executes command strings.

content.value_statement: Developers often need a minimal working Enact skill to verify local setup. This skill runs a small Go program that greets a supplied name and reports the Go runtime version.
actual_capabilities: Accepts a single name input with World as the default value.
Runs a Go source file inside a golang Alpine container.
Prints a greeting that includes the supplied name.
Prints the Go runtime version used during execution.
Provides a compact Enact skill manifest and usage example.
limitations: It only performs greeting output and does not provide broader Go project scaffolding.
It does not validate or sanitize the supplied name before command interpolation.
It requires an Enact-compatible runtime and a Go container environment.
It does not call external services or persist output.

use_cases.title: Validate Enact Setup
use_cases.description: Run a simple skill to confirm that Enact can execute a Go container and return output.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Anatomy
use_cases.description: Show how a manifest, input schema, output schema, and command connect in a small example.

use_cases.title: Smoke Test Automation
use_cases.description: Use a lightweight command to check that skill execution works in a local or CI environment.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Default Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-hello-go with the default name and show me the greeting output.

prompt_templates.title: Greet a Named User
prompt_templates.prompt: Run enact-hello-go with the name Alice and summarize the output in one sentence.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Skill Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect enact-hello-go and explain how its input schema, command, and Go program work together.

prompt_templates.title: Review Command Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review enact-hello-go for command interpolation risk and suggest safer argument handling.

output_examples.output: The tool returns a greeting for Alice and reports the Go version used by the runtime.
The tool uses World as the default name and prints a standard greeting.

output_examples.input: name is a short team label
output_examples.output: The tool includes the supplied label in the greeting and then prints runtime information.

best_practices: Use short alphanumeric names when running the example.
Treat this as a learning and smoke test skill, not a production workflow.
Review command argument handling before adapting the pattern to sensitive inputs.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted shell metacharacters as the name input.
Do not extend the command template with secrets or credentials.
Do not assume this example validates input for production use.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs a small Go program that greets a name and prints the Go runtime version.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it access the network?
faq.answer: No evidence of network access was found in the reviewed files.

faq.question: Does it store files or secrets?
faq.answer: No evidence of persistence, secret handling, or credential access was found.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The command template interpolates user input, so safety depends on runner argument handling.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production input?
faq.answer: It is best used as an example unless command arguments are validated or passed safely.
